cards.text: Malaysia raised its rice self-sufficiency target to 80% by 2030, up from the previous 75%.
cards.sub: The rate it is raising from sits at 56.2%, and it has not been climbing toward the goal.
sources: The Edge (target 80% by 2030, up from 75%); Malay Mail/BERNAMA/The Star (SSR 56.2%, Oct/Nov 2024)

cards.h: The actual self-sufficiency rate is 56.2%. The target rose to 80%; the harvest did not.
cards.s: Closing a gap of nearly 24 points by 2030 needs the rate to climb about four points a year. It has been broadly flat.
cards.d: The actual self-sufficiency rate is 56.2%. The target rose to 80%; the harvest did not. Closing a gap of nearly 24 points by 2030 needs the rate to climb about four points a year. It has been broadly flat.
sources: DOSM SUA 2020-2024; Agriculture Minister Mohamad Sabu, Oct/Nov 2024 (BERNAMA, The Star, Malay Mail)

cards.h: Malaysia buys roughly 44% of its rice abroad, tying the staple to other governments' export decisions.
cards.s: When India, the world's largest rice exporter, curbed shipments in 2023, importing nations including Malaysia faced tighter supply and higher prices.
cards.d: Malaysia buys roughly 44% of its rice abroad, tying the staple to other governments' export decisions. When India, the world's largest rice exporter, curbed shipments in 2023, importing nations including Malaysia faced tighter supply and higher prices.
sources: Import dependence = 100 - SSR 56.2%; India non-basmati white rice export ban, July 2023 (widely documented)

cards.h: The national food import bill nearly doubled in a decade, from RM42.6 billion in 2014 to RM78.8 billion in 2023.
cards.s: That is the gross import bill, not the net food trade deficit of about RM31 billion. Both widened as the ringgit weakened.
cards.d: The national food import bill nearly doubled in a decade, from RM42.6 billion in 2014 to RM78.8 billion in 2023. That is the gross import bill, not the net food trade deficit of about RM31 billion. Both widened as the ringgit weakened.
sources: DOSM External Trade (RM42.64B 2014, RM78.80B 2023); The Malaysian Reserve (net deficit ~RM31B, 2022)

cards.text: Raising the self-sufficiency target raises the ambition, not the harvest. The 24-point gap is closed in the paddy field, not the policy document.
Raising a self-sufficiency target without raising output is like lifting your savings goal without lifting your income. The number on paper changes; the balance in the account does not.
A government can legislate a target but not a harvest. Rice security is won in the granary, not the gazette.

sources: DOSM Supply and Utilisation Accounts 2020-2024; Agriculture Ministry (Mohamad Sabu) Oct/Nov 2024; The Edge; Malay Mail; BERNAMA; NST; DOSM External Trade. See engine/briefs/rice-self-sufficiency-target-gap.md.
confidence: Medium-High. The 56.2% SSR and the 80%-by-2030 (raised from 75%) target are corroborated across multiple outlets citing the minister and DOSM. NOTE: the 62.6% (2022) to 56.2% (2023) drop is partly a methodology change (FAO-aligned, counting only clean padi) plus a real 4.7% production fall; the cards deliberately avoid claiming 'fell 6.4 points' and rest only on the target-vs-actual gap, which holds regardless of method. Primary DOSM SUA PDF 403 to automated fetch; open manually before publishing.
verification_notes: Stage 2 (Bias) and Stage 3 (Fact) reviewers: please (a) confirm SSR 56.2% and the exact year it attaches to against DOSM SUA 2020-2024; (b) confirm the 80%-by-2030 target was raised from 75% and attribute correctly; (c) confirm food import bill RM42.64B (2014) and RM78.80B (2023) against DOSM External Trade; (d) verify the India July 2023 export-restriction framing is not overclaimed for Malaysia specifically. Flag if the methodology-change context (FAO recalculation) needs to appear in a card to avoid misleading framing.
